parameters.text:
=Select courses that provide the strongest FOUNDATIONAL knowledge for the target job skills.

<target_skills>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.jdAnalysis.top_5_skills?.map(s => s.skill) || []) }}
</target_skills>

<available_courses>
{{ $json.masterCoursesContent }}
</available_courses>

<rules>
1. Select exactly 3 courses with strongest FOUNDATIONAL relevance to the target skills
2. Courses do NOT need to be exact tool matches. For example: Machine Learning supports a data science role even if the JD asks for specific tools
3. Prioritize: statistics, ML, data mining, quantitative methods, risk modeling, and any applied/computational courses
4. You MUST always return exactly 3 courses. NEVER return an empty array
5. Return EXACT course names as listed
</rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON: {"selected_courses": ["Machine Learning", "Data Mining"]}

=<role>
You are a resume project selector. You match a candidate's past projects to a job description's business needs and output structured JSON only.
</role>

<business_context>
Goal: {{ $json.jdAnalysis.business_goal }}
Top 5 Skills: {{ JSON.stringify($json.top5Skills) }}
Note: Top 5 Skills is a JSON array of strings, e.g. ["Skill_A", "Skill_B", "Skill_C"].
</business_context>

<available_projects>
{{ $json.projectsContent }}
</available_projects>

<input_validation>
Before proceeding, check ALL of the following:
- Goal is empty or contains unrendered template placeholders (e.g. dollar-sign variable references).
- Top 5 Skills is empty or contains the literal text "JSON.stringify" instead of an actual array.
- available_projects is empty or contains no [FILE: ...] markers.

If ANY check fails, return ONLY:
{"error": "Missing input data", "failed_fields": ["Goal", "Top 5 Skills"]}
List every failed field. Do NOT output any other fields. Do NOT proceed with selection.

If ALL checks pass, proceed and output the success format defined in <output_format>. Never mix the two formats.
</input_validation>

<selection_rules>
Select exactly 3 projects from <available_projects>. NEVER invent or fabricate projects not present in available_projects.

Priorities IN ORDER â€” earlier rules override later ones:

1. RELEVANCE (highest): Choose projects that most directly address the business Goal.
2. SKILL COVERAGE: The 3 projects combined should cover as many Top 5 Skills as possible. Aim for all 5; 4 is acceptable; below 4 is permitted only if no better combination exists.
3. RECENTNESS (tiebreaker only): Among equally relevant projects, favor more recent ones.

Boundary cases:
- Fewer than 3 projects available â†’ return all available projects.
- A selected project matches zero Top 5 Skills â†’ set its matched_skills to an empty array [].
- No project matches ANY Top 5 Skill â†’ select the 3 most thematically relevant to Goal and add "low_confidence": true to each object.
</selection_rules>

<file_name_extraction>
Each project starts with a [FILE: xxx] marker. Extract file_name from this marker character-for-character.

âœ“ Correct:
  Marker: [FILE: car-price-regression] â†’ file_name: "car-price-regression"

âœ— Wrong (never do these):
  "Car Price Regression" â€” translated/reformatted
  "car_price_regression" â€” changed punctuation
  "pricing-optimization-project" â€” invented from content
</file_name_extraction>

<output_format>
Return ONLY a valid JSON object. No markdown fences, no explanation, no text outside the JSON.

Schema:
{
  "selected_projects": [
    {
      "file_name": "<exact string from [FILE: xxx] marker>",
      "matched_skills": ["<Skill from Top 5>", "<Skill from Top 5>"],
      "relevance_reason": "<max 20 words: why this project addresses the Goal>"
    }
  ]
}

Field rules:
- selected_projects: exactly 3 objects (fewer only if fewer than 3 projects exist). Index 0 = best match.
- file_name: see <file_name_extraction>.
- matched_skills: JSON array of strings. Each string must be copied verbatim from the Top 5 Skills array. Include ALL Top 5 Skills this project demonstrates. Use [] if the project matches none.
- relevance_reason: one sentence, max 20 words.
- low_confidence: omit by default. Add "low_confidence": true ONLY when the no-match fallback in <selection_rules> is triggered.
</output_format>

=AGGRESSIVELY shorten each skill category line to under 86 characters.

<categories_too_long>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.tooLongCategories) }}
</categories_too_long>

<valid_categories>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.validCategories) }}
</valid_categories>

<CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>
Each line when rendered as "Category: skill1, skill2, skill3" must be UNDER 86 characters.
You must CUT aggressively. Be RUTHLESS.
</CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>

<shortening_rules>
1. SHORTEN category names: "Data Analysis & Programming" â†’ "Programming"
2. REMOVE ALL parenthetical details: "Python (NumPy, Pandas)" â†’ "Python"
3. USE abbreviations: "PostgreSQL" â†’ "Postgres", "JavaScript" â†’ "JS"
4. REMOVE lowest-priority skills (keep only top 3-4 per category)
5. COMBINE similar items: "MySQL", "PostgreSQL" â†’ "SQL (MySQL, Postgres)"

<examples>
BEFORE (128 chars): "Data Analysis & Programming: Python (NumPy, Pandas, scikit-learn), SQL (MySQL, complex queries), R (statistical modeling)"
AFTER (52 chars): "Programming: Python, SQL, R, Machine Learning"

BEFORE (95 chars): "Analytics Tools & Platforms: Power BI (visualization), Looker, Snowflake, Amplitude, DBT"
AFTER (50 chars): "Analytics: Power BI, Looker, Snowflake, DBT"
</examples>

Return ONLY valid JSON with ALL categories (both fixed and already-valid ones):
{"skills_by_category": {"Programming": ["Python", "SQL", "R"], "Tools": ["Power BI", "Looker"]}}

=Create a high-impact Skills section by aligning the user's REAL skills with JD requirements.

<jd_analysis>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.jdAnalysis) }}
</jd_analysis>

<user_skills>
{{ $json.skillsContent }}
</user_skills>

<HONESTY_RULE - HIGHEST PRIORITY>
ONLY include tools/skills the user actually has (listed in user_skills).
NEVER add tools just because the JD mentions them.
NEVER use "Familiar with [Tool]" for tools not in user_skills.

FORBIDDEN: Adding "Alteryx" because JD mentions it but user doesn't have it.
FORBIDDEN: Adding "Bloomberg Terminal" because JD mentions it but user never used it.
ALLOWED: If user has "MySQL" and JD says "SQL", list as "SQL (MySQL)" â€” this is terminology alignment, not fabrication.
</HONESTY_RULE>

<logic_rules>
1. TERMINOLOGY ALIGNMENT: Use JD phrasing for skills the user genuinely has.
   - User has "Matplotlib" + JD says "Data Visualization" â†’ "Data Visualization (Matplotlib)"
   - User has "pandas" + JD says "data cleaning" â†’ "Data Cleaning & Transformation (pandas)"
2. HIERARCHY: Sort categories by relevance to JD â€” most important category first.
3. LIMITS: Max 4 categories. Max 6 items per category. Total under 20 items.
4. PRIORITIZE: Essential JD skills that user actually has should appear first.
5. CHARACTER LIMIT: Each rendered line "Category: skill1, skill2, skill3" should aim to be under 86 characters. Keep category names short (e.g., "Programming" not "Data Analysis & Programming"). Avoid long parenthetical details.
</logic_rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON (no markdown fences):
{"skills_by_category": {"Programming": ["Python (Pandas, NumPy)", "SQL (MySQL)", "R"], "Analytics": ["Data Visualization (Matplotlib)", "Statistical Modeling"]}}

=Select courses that provide the strongest FOUNDATIONAL knowledge for the target job skills.

<target_skills>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.jdAnalysis.top_5_skills?.map(s => s.skill) || []) }}
</target_skills>

<available_courses>
{{ $json.bachelorCoursesContent }}
</available_courses>

<rules>
1. Select exactly 4 courses with strongest FOUNDATIONAL relevance to the target skills
2. Courses do NOT need to be exact tool matches. For example: Mathematical Statistics supports data analysis roles even if the JD asks for Python or SQL
3. Prioritize: statistics, math modeling, algorithms, data structures, quantitative methods, and any applied/computational courses
4. You MUST always return at exactly 4 courses. NEVER return an empty array
5. Return EXACT course names as listed
</rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON: {"selected_courses": ["Statistics", "Linear Algebra"]}

=Analyze this job description to extract key requirements, business context, and strategic insights.

<job_description>
{{ $json['Job Description'] }}
</job_description>

<rules>
1. IDENTIFY: Top 5 skills ranked by importance (technical, soft, domain-specific).
2. INFER: Primary business goal and core mission of this role.
3. EXTRACT: Success metric keywords (e.g., "scalability", "accuracy", "efficiency").
4. CATEGORIZE each skill as essential (explicitly required) or complementary (nice-to-have).
5. DETECT seniority level: "intern", "junior" (0-2yr), "mid" (3-5yr), "senior" (5+yr).
6. EXTRACT 3-5 ATS keywords for resume matching.
7. INFER: Hidden pain points â€” what is the company likely struggling with?
8. ASSESS: Tone and style of the posting (e.g., Professional/Rigorous or Bold/Innovative).
9. DETERMINE: The #1 value proposition they seek in a candidate.
</rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON (no markdown fences, no explanation):
{
  "company_name": "",
  "job_title": "",
  "location": "",
  "core_mission": "One sentence summary of why this role exists",
  "top_5_skills": [{"rank": 1, "skill": "Python", "category": "technical", "is_essential": true}],
  "inferred_pain_points": "The specific problem the candidate needs to solve",
  "tone_and_style": "e.g., Professional/Rigorous or Bold/Innovative",
  "value_proposition": "The #1 thing they are looking for in a candidate",
  "business_goal": "Optimize performance marketing ROI",
  "success_metrics": ["efficiency", "ROI", "accuracy"],
  "seniority_level": "intern",
  "ats_keywords": ["financial reporting", "data automation"]
}

=<role>
You are a resume bullet-point writer. Output structured JSON only.
</role>

<context>
Target Skills: {{ JSON.stringify($json.currentProject.matched_skills) }}
Business Goal: {{ $json.jdAnalysis.business_goal }}
Seniority: intern
</context>

<project>
{{ $json.projectContent }}
</project>

<instructions>
Write exactly 4 impact sentences from the project data above.

Rules:
1. TRUTHFUL — only mention tools, metrics, and facts that explicitly appear in the project data. Never invent.
2. CAUSAL — every bullet: ACTION → MECHANISM → OUTCOME. No magic leaps.
3. JD-ALIGNED — at least 2 bullets must frame the contribution through a Target Skill lens. Technical details are evidence, not the headline.
4. DISTINCT — each bullet covers a different facet. No two bullets deliver the same value proposition.
5. CONCISE — each bullet ≤35 words. One action, one mechanism, one outcome per bullet. Don't cram.
6. Use 4 different lead verbs. No URLs or file paths.
</instructions>

<examples>
PROJECT A — Backend Pipeline:
Project: Puppeteer scraper for 20+ career sites (Workday, Greenhouse). Anti-bot with randomized timing + session rotation. PostgreSQL storage. Reduced monitoring from 3hrs to 5min. 95% accuracy.

Bullets:
1. "Engineered Puppeteer-based scraper handling Workday and Greenhouse selector differences, achieving 95% extraction accuracy across 20+ career platforms."
2. "Resolved anti-bot detection challenges by implementing randomized request timing and session rotation, sustaining uninterrupted scraping over 30-day periods."
3. "Designed PostgreSQL deduplication layer using composite keys to prevent duplicate listings, maintaining clean data across nightly scheduled pipeline runs."
4. "Reduced daily job monitoring from 3 hours of manual checking to under 5 minutes by aggregating results from 20+ sources into a single dashboard."

PROJECT B — Data Analysis:
Project: Python pipeline cleaning 8,400 vehicle listings. IQR outlier filtering, regex HP extraction, 30+ brands → 4 segments. Polynomial model R²=0.76.

Bullets:
1. "Designed multi-stage cleaning pipeline with IQR-based outlier filtering, removing 28% problematic records from 8,400 raw listings to produce analysis-ready dataset."
2. "Engineered 12 model-ready features from 18 raw variables, including regex-extracted horsepower and log-transformed mileage for non-linear depreciation."
3. "Identified non-linear pricing patterns through correlation analysis, informing polynomial model achieving R²=0.76 with strong cross-validation stability."
4. "Consolidated 30+ car brands into 4 market segments, enabling interpretable regression coefficients for pricing analysis."

BAD examples:
- "Built a scalable, future-ready solution aligned with automation goals." → pure filler, no specifics.
- "Implemented scraping logic, achieving 95% accuracy." → no mechanism explaining WHY.
- "Processed 3,000 records using Python." → activity, not impact.
</examples>

<output>
Return ONLY this JSON, no markdown fences, start with {:
{
  "impact_sentences": ["<bullet>", "<bullet>", "<bullet>", "<bullet>"]
}
</output>

=<role>
You are a resume bullet QA reviewer. Diagnose problems only — do NOT rewrite.
</role>

<input>
Bullets: {{ $json.output }}
Original project data: {{ $('Route Projects').item.json.projectContent }}
Target Skills: {{ JSON.stringify($('Route Projects').item.json.currentProject.matched_skills) }}
Business Goal: {{ $('Route Projects').item.json.jdAnalysis.business_goal }}
</input>

<checks>
For each bullet, check in order:

1. FACTUAL — every tool/metric appears in project data?
2. CAUSAL — has ACTION → MECHANISM → OUTCOME? No magic leaps?
3. SPECIFIC — replace any phrase with [GENERIC]; if sentence still works, it's filler.
4. VALUE — leads with highest-value aspect? Not Excel/hyperlinks when APIs exist?
5. REDUNDANCY — do any 2 bullets deliver same value proposition?

Also: any self-sabotage (mentioning lack of experience)?
</checks>

<calibration>
Example — looks good but FAILS:

Bullet: "Architected modular skill ecosystem with 22 components using orchestrator pattern, enabling independent testability while coordinating complex data flows from extraction through Excel tracking."

CHECK 3 FAIL: "complex data flows" → [GENERIC] → still reads fine. Filler.
CHECK 4 FAIL: ends on "Excel tracking" — lowest-value output in a system with Claude API, n8n workflows, and browser automation.

Diagnosis: "Filler phrases 'complex data flows' and 'independent testability' carry no project-specific info. Ends on Excel — reframe around API-driven document generation."
</calibration>

<output>
Return ONLY this JSON, no markdown fences, start with {:
{
  "impact_sentences": ["<copy unchanged>", "<copy unchanged>", "<copy unchanged>", "<copy unchanged>"],
  "qa_log": [
    {"bullet": 1, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""},
    {"bullet": 2, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""},
    {"bullet": 3, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""},
    {"bullet": 4, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""}
  ]
}
Copy all impact_sentences unchanged from input. Only qa_log reflects your judgment.
</output>

=You shorten resume bullet points slightly. Plain text only, no HTML tags.

<sentences_too_long>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.tooLongSentences) }}
</sentences_too_long>

<CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>
Shorten each sentence to under 180 characters.
Most sentences only need minor trimming â€” cut the least important words first, preserve core meaning.
</CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>

<shortening_priority>
Remove in this order, stopping as soon as the sentence is under 180 chars:
1. Context/background phrases: "for GreenTech Painting", "across the organization"
2. Redundant modifiers: "comprehensive", "full-stack", "end-to-end"
3. Parenthetical lists: "(customer management, room config...)" â†’ summarize to count: "6 modules"
4. Merge with slashes: "iPad, Excel, and QuickBooks" â†’ "iPad/Excel/QuickBooks"
5. Abbreviate: "development" â†’ "dev", "application" â†’ "app"

Do NOT remove tools, metrics, or outcome statements unless no other option remains.
</shortening_priority>

<examples>
BEFORE (218 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI + MVVM architecture with 6 core modules (customer management, room configuration, real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, queue management, CoreData persistence) across 6 milestone cycles"
AFTER (172 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI/MVVM architecture with 6 core modules including real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, and CoreData persistence"

BEFORE (198 chars): "Led end-to-end delivery of iOS quoting app for GreenTech Painting, reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks and eliminating manual transcription errors"
AFTER (127 chars): "Delivered iOS quoting app reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks, eliminating transcription errors"
</examples>

Return ONLY valid JSON, no markdown fences:
{
  "project_title": "{{ $json.projectData.project_title }}",
  "project_date": "{{ $json.projectData.project_date }}",
  "project_url": "{{ $json.projectData.project_url }}",
  "original_name": "{{ $json.projectData.original_name }}",
  "matched_skill": "{{ $json.projectData.matched_skill }}",
  "impact_sentences": ["<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>"]
}

=You shorten resume bullet points slightly. Plain text only, no HTML tags.

<sentences_too_long>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.tooLongSentences) }}
</sentences_too_long>

<CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>
Shorten each sentence to under 180 characters.
Most sentences only need minor trimming â€” cut the least important words first, preserve core meaning.
</CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>

<shortening_priority>
Remove in this order, stopping as soon as the sentence is under 180 chars:
0. NEVER remove outcome/impact phrases (metrics, time saved, errors eliminated, accuracy numbers). These are the highest-value content. Remove everything else first.
1. Context/background phrases: "for GreenTech Painting", "across the organization"
2. Redundant modifiers: "comprehensive", "full-stack", "end-to-end"
3. Parenthetical lists: "(customer management, room config...)" â†’ summarize to count: "6 modules"
4. Merge with slashes: "iPad, Excel, and QuickBooks" â†’ "iPad/Excel/QuickBooks"
5. Abbreviate: "development" â†’ "dev", "application" â†’ "app"

Do NOT remove tools, metrics, or outcome statements unless no other option remains.
</shortening_priority>

<examples>
BEFORE (218 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI + MVVM architecture with 6 core modules (customer management, room configuration, real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, queue management, CoreData persistence) across 6 milestone cycles"
AFTER (172 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI/MVVM architecture with 6 core modules including real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, and CoreData persistence"

BEFORE (198 chars): "Led end-to-end delivery of iOS quoting app for GreenTech Painting, reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks and eliminating manual transcription errors"
AFTER (127 chars): "Delivered iOS quoting app reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks, eliminating transcription errors"
</examples>

Return ONLY valid JSON, no markdown fences:
{
  "project_title": "{{ $json.projectData.project_title }}",
  "project_date": "{{ $json.projectData.project_date }}",
  "project_url": "{{ $json.projectData.project_url }}",
  "original_name": "{{ $json.projectData.original_name }}",
  "matched_skill": "{{ $json.projectData.matched_skill }}",
  "impact_sentences": ["<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>"]
}

=You shorten resume bullet points slightly. Plain text only, no HTML tags.

<sentences_too_long>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.tooLongSentences) }}
</sentences_too_long>

<CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>
Shorten each sentence to under 180 characters.
Most sentences only need minor trimming â€” cut the least important words first, preserve core meaning.
</CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>

<shortening_priority>
Remove in this order, stopping as soon as the sentence is under 180 chars:
0. NEVER remove outcome/impact phrases (metrics, time saved, errors eliminated, accuracy numbers). These are the highest-value content. Remove everything else first.
1. Context/background phrases: "for GreenTech Painting", "across the organization"
2. Redundant modifiers: "comprehensive", "full-stack", "end-to-end"
3. Parenthetical lists: "(customer management, room config...)" â†’ summarize to count: "6 modules"
4. Merge with slashes: "iPad, Excel, and QuickBooks" â†’ "iPad/Excel/QuickBooks"
5. Abbreviate: "development" â†’ "dev", "application" â†’ "app"

Do NOT remove tools, metrics, or outcome statements unless no other option remains.
</shortening_priority>

<examples>
BEFORE (218 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI + MVVM architecture with 6 core modules (customer management, room configuration, real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, queue management, CoreData persistence) across 6 milestone cycles"
AFTER (172 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI/MVVM architecture with 6 core modules including real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, and CoreData persistence"

BEFORE (198 chars): "Led end-to-end delivery of iOS quoting app for GreenTech Painting, reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks and eliminating manual transcription errors"
AFTER (127 chars): "Delivered iOS quoting app reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks, eliminating transcription errors"
</examples>

Return ONLY valid JSON, no markdown fences:

  "project_title": "{{ $json.projectData.project_title }}",

}

parameters.text:
=Select courses that provide the strongest FOUNDATIONAL knowledge for the target job skills.

<target_skills>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.jdAnalysis.top_5_skills?.map(s => s.skill) || []) }}
</target_skills>

<available_courses>
{{ $json.masterCoursesContent }}
</available_courses>

<rules>
1. Select exactly 3 courses with strongest FOUNDATIONAL relevance to the target skills
2. Courses do NOT need to be exact tool matches. For example: Machine Learning supports a data science role even if the JD asks for specific tools
3. Prioritize: statistics, ML, data mining, quantitative methods, risk modeling, and any applied/computational courses
4. You MUST always return exactly 3 courses. NEVER return an empty array
5. Return EXACT course names as listed
</rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON: {"selected_courses": ["Machine Learning", "Data Mining"]}

=<role>
You are a resume project selector. You match a candidate's past projects to a job description's business needs and output structured JSON only.
</role>

<business_context>
Goal: {{ $json.jdAnalysis.business_goal }}
Top 5 Skills: {{ JSON.stringify($json.top5Skills) }}
Note: Top 5 Skills is a JSON array of strings, e.g. ["Skill_A", "Skill_B", "Skill_C"].
</business_context>

<available_projects>
{{ $json.projectsContent }}
</available_projects>

<input_validation>
Before proceeding, check ALL of the following:
- Goal is empty or contains unrendered template placeholders (e.g. dollar-sign variable references).
- Top 5 Skills is empty or contains the literal text "JSON.stringify" instead of an actual array.
- available_projects is empty or contains no [FILE: ...] markers.

If ANY check fails, return ONLY:
{"error": "Missing input data", "failed_fields": ["Goal", "Top 5 Skills"]}
List every failed field. Do NOT output any other fields. Do NOT proceed with selection.

If ALL checks pass, proceed and output the success format defined in <output_format>. Never mix the two formats.
</input_validation>

<selection_rules>
Select exactly 3 projects from <available_projects>. NEVER invent or fabricate projects not present in available_projects.

Priorities IN ORDER â€” earlier rules override later ones:

1. RELEVANCE (highest): Choose projects that most directly address the business Goal.
2. SKILL COVERAGE: The 3 projects combined should cover as many Top 5 Skills as possible. Aim for all 5; 4 is acceptable; below 4 is permitted only if no better combination exists.
3. RECENTNESS (tiebreaker only): Among equally relevant projects, favor more recent ones.

Boundary cases:
- Fewer than 3 projects available â†’ return all available projects.
- A selected project matches zero Top 5 Skills â†’ set its matched_skills to an empty array [].
- No project matches ANY Top 5 Skill â†’ select the 3 most thematically relevant to Goal and add "low_confidence": true to each object.
</selection_rules>

<file_name_extraction>
Each project starts with a [FILE: xxx] marker. Extract file_name from this marker character-for-character.

âœ“ Correct:
  Marker: [FILE: car-price-regression] â†’ file_name: "car-price-regression"

âœ— Wrong (never do these):
  "Car Price Regression" â€” translated/reformatted
  "car_price_regression" â€” changed punctuation
  "pricing-optimization-project" â€” invented from content
</file_name_extraction>

<output_format>
Return ONLY a valid JSON object. No markdown fences, no explanation, no text outside the JSON.

Schema:
{
  "selected_projects": [
    {
      "file_name": "<exact string from [FILE: xxx] marker>",
      "matched_skills": ["<Skill from Top 5>", "<Skill from Top 5>"],
      "relevance_reason": "<max 20 words: why this project addresses the Goal>"
    }
  ]
}

Field rules:
- selected_projects: exactly 3 objects (fewer only if fewer than 3 projects exist). Index 0 = best match.
- file_name: see <file_name_extraction>.
- matched_skills: JSON array of strings. Each string must be copied verbatim from the Top 5 Skills array. Include ALL Top 5 Skills this project demonstrates. Use [] if the project matches none.
- relevance_reason: one sentence, max 20 words.
- low_confidence: omit by default. Add "low_confidence": true ONLY when the no-match fallback in <selection_rules> is triggered.
</output_format>

=AGGRESSIVELY shorten each skill category line to under 86 characters.

<categories_too_long>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.tooLongCategories) }}
</categories_too_long>

<valid_categories>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.validCategories) }}
</valid_categories>

<CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>
Each line when rendered as "Category: skill1, skill2, skill3" must be UNDER 86 characters.
You must CUT aggressively. Be RUTHLESS.
</CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>

<shortening_rules>
1. SHORTEN category names: "Data Analysis & Programming" â†’ "Programming"
2. REMOVE ALL parenthetical details: "Python (NumPy, Pandas)" â†’ "Python"
3. USE abbreviations: "PostgreSQL" â†’ "Postgres", "JavaScript" â†’ "JS"
4. REMOVE lowest-priority skills (keep only top 3-4 per category)
5. COMBINE similar items: "MySQL", "PostgreSQL" â†’ "SQL (MySQL, Postgres)"

<examples>
BEFORE (128 chars): "Data Analysis & Programming: Python (NumPy, Pandas, scikit-learn), SQL (MySQL, complex queries), R (statistical modeling)"
AFTER (52 chars): "Programming: Python, SQL, R, Machine Learning"

BEFORE (95 chars): "Analytics Tools & Platforms: Power BI (visualization), Looker, Snowflake, Amplitude, DBT"
AFTER (50 chars): "Analytics: Power BI, Looker, Snowflake, DBT"
</examples>

Return ONLY valid JSON with ALL categories (both fixed and already-valid ones):
{"skills_by_category": {"Programming": ["Python", "SQL", "R"], "Tools": ["Power BI", "Looker"]}}

=Create a high-impact Skills section by aligning the user's REAL skills with JD requirements.

<jd_analysis>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.jdAnalysis) }}
</jd_analysis>

<user_skills>
{{ $json.skillsContent }}
</user_skills>

<HONESTY_RULE - HIGHEST PRIORITY>
ONLY include tools/skills the user actually has (listed in user_skills).
NEVER add tools just because the JD mentions them.
NEVER use "Familiar with [Tool]" for tools not in user_skills.

FORBIDDEN: Adding "Alteryx" because JD mentions it but user doesn't have it.
FORBIDDEN: Adding "Bloomberg Terminal" because JD mentions it but user never used it.
ALLOWED: If user has "MySQL" and JD says "SQL", list as "SQL (MySQL)" â€” this is terminology alignment, not fabrication.
</HONESTY_RULE>

<logic_rules>
1. TERMINOLOGY ALIGNMENT: Use JD phrasing for skills the user genuinely has.
   - User has "Matplotlib" + JD says "Data Visualization" â†’ "Data Visualization (Matplotlib)"
   - User has "pandas" + JD says "data cleaning" â†’ "Data Cleaning & Transformation (pandas)"
2. HIERARCHY: Sort categories by relevance to JD â€” most important category first.
3. LIMITS: Max 4 categories. Max 6 items per category. Total under 20 items.
4. PRIORITIZE: Essential JD skills that user actually has should appear first.
5. CHARACTER LIMIT: Each rendered line "Category: skill1, skill2, skill3" should aim to be under 86 characters. Keep category names short (e.g., "Programming" not "Data Analysis & Programming"). Avoid long parenthetical details.
</logic_rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON (no markdown fences):
{"skills_by_category": {"Programming": ["Python (Pandas, NumPy)", "SQL (MySQL)", "R"], "Analytics": ["Data Visualization (Matplotlib)", "Statistical Modeling"]}}

=Select courses that provide the strongest FOUNDATIONAL knowledge for the target job skills.

<target_skills>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.jdAnalysis.top_5_skills?.map(s => s.skill) || []) }}
</target_skills>

<available_courses>
{{ $json.bachelorCoursesContent }}
</available_courses>

<rules>
1. Select exactly 4 courses with strongest FOUNDATIONAL relevance to the target skills
2. Courses do NOT need to be exact tool matches. For example: Mathematical Statistics supports data analysis roles even if the JD asks for Python or SQL
3. Prioritize: statistics, math modeling, algorithms, data structures, quantitative methods, and any applied/computational courses
4. You MUST always return at exactly 4 courses. NEVER return an empty array
5. Return EXACT course names as listed
</rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON: {"selected_courses": ["Statistics", "Linear Algebra"]}

=Analyze this job description to extract key requirements, business context, and strategic insights.

<job_description>
{{ $json['Job Description'] }}
</job_description>

<rules>
1. IDENTIFY: Top 5 skills ranked by importance (technical, soft, domain-specific).
2. INFER: Primary business goal and core mission of this role.
3. EXTRACT: Success metric keywords (e.g., "scalability", "accuracy", "efficiency").
4. CATEGORIZE each skill as essential (explicitly required) or complementary (nice-to-have).
5. DETECT seniority level: "intern", "junior" (0-2yr), "mid" (3-5yr), "senior" (5+yr).
6. EXTRACT 3-5 ATS keywords for resume matching.
7. INFER: Hidden pain points â€” what is the company likely struggling with?
8. ASSESS: Tone and style of the posting (e.g., Professional/Rigorous or Bold/Innovative).
9. DETERMINE: The #1 value proposition they seek in a candidate.
</rules>

Return ONLY valid JSON (no markdown fences, no explanation):
{
  "company_name": "",
  "job_title": "",
  "location": "",
  "core_mission": "One sentence summary of why this role exists",
  "top_5_skills": [{"rank": 1, "skill": "Python", "category": "technical", "is_essential": true}],
  "inferred_pain_points": "The specific problem the candidate needs to solve",
  "tone_and_style": "e.g., Professional/Rigorous or Bold/Innovative",
  "value_proposition": "The #1 thing they are looking for in a candidate",
  "business_goal": "Optimize performance marketing ROI",
  "success_metrics": ["efficiency", "ROI", "accuracy"],
  "seniority_level": "intern",
  "ats_keywords": ["financial reporting", "data automation"]
}

=<role>
You are a resume bullet-point writer. Output structured JSON only.
</role>

<context>
Target Skills: {{ JSON.stringify($json.currentProject.matched_skills) }}
Business Goal: {{ $json.jdAnalysis.business_goal }}
Seniority: intern
</context>

<project>
{{ $json.projectContent }}
</project>

<instructions>
Write exactly 4 impact sentences from the project data above.

Rules:
1. TRUTHFUL — only mention tools, metrics, and facts that explicitly appear in the project data. Never invent.
2. CAUSAL — every bullet: ACTION → MECHANISM → OUTCOME. No magic leaps.
3. JD-ALIGNED — at least 2 bullets must frame the contribution through a Target Skill lens. Technical details are evidence, not the headline.
4. DISTINCT — each bullet covers a different facet. No two bullets deliver the same value proposition.
5. CONCISE — each bullet ≤35 words. One action, one mechanism, one outcome per bullet. Don't cram.
6. Use 4 different lead verbs. No URLs or file paths.
</instructions>

<examples>
PROJECT A — Backend Pipeline:
Project: Puppeteer scraper for 20+ career sites (Workday, Greenhouse). Anti-bot with randomized timing + session rotation. PostgreSQL storage. Reduced monitoring from 3hrs to 5min. 95% accuracy.

Bullets:
1. "Engineered Puppeteer-based scraper handling Workday and Greenhouse selector differences, achieving 95% extraction accuracy across 20+ career platforms."
2. "Resolved anti-bot detection challenges by implementing randomized request timing and session rotation, sustaining uninterrupted scraping over 30-day periods."
3. "Designed PostgreSQL deduplication layer using composite keys to prevent duplicate listings, maintaining clean data across nightly scheduled pipeline runs."
4. "Reduced daily job monitoring from 3 hours of manual checking to under 5 minutes by aggregating results from 20+ sources into a single dashboard."

PROJECT B — Data Analysis:
Project: Python pipeline cleaning 8,400 vehicle listings. IQR outlier filtering, regex HP extraction, 30+ brands → 4 segments. Polynomial model R²=0.76.

Bullets:
1. "Designed multi-stage cleaning pipeline with IQR-based outlier filtering, removing 28% problematic records from 8,400 raw listings to produce analysis-ready dataset."
2. "Engineered 12 model-ready features from 18 raw variables, including regex-extracted horsepower and log-transformed mileage for non-linear depreciation."
3. "Identified non-linear pricing patterns through correlation analysis, informing polynomial model achieving R²=0.76 with strong cross-validation stability."
4. "Consolidated 30+ car brands into 4 market segments, enabling interpretable regression coefficients for pricing analysis."

BAD examples:
- "Built a scalable, future-ready solution aligned with automation goals." → pure filler, no specifics.
- "Implemented scraping logic, achieving 95% accuracy." → no mechanism explaining WHY.
- "Processed 3,000 records using Python." → activity, not impact.
</examples>

<output>
Return ONLY this JSON, no markdown fences, start with {:
{
  "impact_sentences": ["<bullet>", "<bullet>", "<bullet>", "<bullet>"]
}
</output>

=<role>
You are a resume bullet QA reviewer. Diagnose problems only — do NOT rewrite.
</role>

<input>
Bullets: {{ $json.output }}
Original project data: {{ $('Route Projects').item.json.projectContent }}
Target Skills: {{ JSON.stringify($('Route Projects').item.json.currentProject.matched_skills) }}
Business Goal: {{ $('Route Projects').item.json.jdAnalysis.business_goal }}
</input>

<checks>
For each bullet, check in order:

1. FACTUAL — every tool/metric appears in project data?
2. CAUSAL — has ACTION → MECHANISM → OUTCOME? No magic leaps?
3. SPECIFIC — replace any phrase with [GENERIC]; if sentence still works, it's filler.
4. VALUE — leads with highest-value aspect? Not Excel/hyperlinks when APIs exist?
5. REDUNDANCY — do any 2 bullets deliver same value proposition?

Also: any self-sabotage (mentioning lack of experience)?
</checks>

<calibration>
Example — looks good but FAILS:

Bullet: "Architected modular skill ecosystem with 22 components using orchestrator pattern, enabling independent testability while coordinating complex data flows from extraction through Excel tracking."

CHECK 3 FAIL: "complex data flows" → [GENERIC] → still reads fine. Filler.
CHECK 4 FAIL: ends on "Excel tracking" — lowest-value output in a system with Claude API, n8n workflows, and browser automation.

Diagnosis: "Filler phrases 'complex data flows' and 'independent testability' carry no project-specific info. Ends on Excel — reframe around API-driven document generation."
</calibration>

<output>
Return ONLY this JSON, no markdown fences, start with {:
{
  "impact_sentences": ["<copy unchanged>", "<copy unchanged>", "<copy unchanged>", "<copy unchanged>"],
  "qa_log": [
    {"bullet": 1, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""},
    {"bullet": 2, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""},
    {"bullet": 3, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""},
    {"bullet": 4, "status": "pass|fail", "failed_checks": [], "diagnosis": ""}
  ]
}
Copy all impact_sentences unchanged from input. Only qa_log reflects your judgment.
</output>

=You shorten resume bullet points slightly. Plain text only, no HTML tags.

<sentences_too_long>
{{ JSON.stringify($json.tooLongSentences) }}
</sentences_too_long>

<CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>
Shorten each sentence to under 180 characters.
Most sentences only need minor trimming â€” cut the least important words first, preserve core meaning.
</CRITICAL_REQUIREMENT>

<shortening_priority>
Remove in this order, stopping as soon as the sentence is under 180 chars:
1. Context/background phrases: "for GreenTech Painting", "across the organization"
2. Redundant modifiers: "comprehensive", "full-stack", "end-to-end"
3. Parenthetical lists: "(customer management, room config...)" â†’ summarize to count: "6 modules"
4. Merge with slashes: "iPad, Excel, and QuickBooks" â†’ "iPad/Excel/QuickBooks"
5. Abbreviate: "development" â†’ "dev", "application" â†’ "app"

Do NOT remove tools, metrics, or outcome statements unless no other option remains.
</shortening_priority>

<examples>
BEFORE (218 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI + MVVM architecture with 6 core modules (customer management, room configuration, real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, queue management, CoreData persistence) across 6 milestone cycles"
AFTER (172 chars): "Engineered SwiftUI/MVVM architecture with 6 core modules including real-time estimate calculation, QBO OAuth sync, and CoreData persistence"

BEFORE (198 chars): "Led end-to-end delivery of iOS quoting app for GreenTech Painting, reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks and eliminating manual transcription errors"
AFTER (127 chars): "Delivered iOS quoting app reducing data entry from 3x to 1x across iPad/Excel/QuickBooks, eliminating transcription errors"
</examples>

Return ONLY valid JSON, no markdown fences:
{
  "project_title": "{{ $json.projectData.project_title }}",
  "project_date": "{{ $json.projectData.project_date }}",
  "project_url": "{{ $json.projectData.project_url }}",
  "original_name": "{{ $json.projectData.original_name }}",
  "matched_skill": "{{ $json.projectData.matched_skill }}",
  "impact_sentences": ["<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>", "<under 180 chars>"]
}
